coredata.dc:title: Investigation of the photocatalytic properties of Ir(III) complex-graphene quantum dot nanocomposites using density functional theory calculations
coredata.dc:creator: Cui, Peng
Zhang, Jian
coredata.dc:description: The current study investigates the photocatalytic properties of Ir(III) complex–graphene quantum dot (GQD) nanocomposites for potential applications in photocatalysis using density functional theory calculations. The 2-aryl substituent with strong electron-accepting strength leads to better charge transfer ability of the nanocomposite, while the one with strong electron-donating strength redshifts absorption peaks. The energy alignment between the Ir(III) complex and the GQD creates favorable conditions for charge injection to TiO2. Moreover, the electron-donating substituent increases the delocalization of π-electrons in the nanocomposite, enhancing the number of charge carriers injected to TiO2. Furthermore, the electron-donating group increases the charge carrier density of excited states and thus the light collection efficiency (LHE) of the nanocomposite. In contrast, the electron-withdrawing group reduces the LHE and thus the photoelectric conversion of the nanocomposite. The current study depicts the detailed role of electronegativity of the substituted functional group on the energy conversion process of Ir(III) complex–GQD nanocomposites.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ir(III) complex–GQD nanocomposite
Energy conversion efficiency
Photoelectric performance
Nonlinear optics

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representations of Ir(III) complex–GQD nanocomposites with different electron-donating and -withdrawing groups at the 2-position of the oxazole ring of oxazolo[4,5-f][1,10]phenanthroline (i.e., 2-aryl substituent). R = 2-(4-nitrophenyl), 2-(4-bromophenyl), 2-(4-diphenylaminophenyl), 2-(4-aminophenyl), 2-(pyridin-4-yl) and 2-phenyl, respectively, where 2-(4-nitrophenyl), 2-(4-bromophenyl) and 2-(pyridin-4-yl) are electron-withdrawing groups and the others are electron-donating groups.

Highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) energy levels of Ir(III) complex–GQD nanocomposites with various 2-aryl substituents. The orange and cyan dashed lines represent the conduction band minimum (CBM) of TiO2 and HOMO of redox couple I−/I3−. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Projected density of states (PDOS) of Ir(III) complex–GQD nanocomposites with different 2-aryl substituents, i.e., (a) R1: 2-phenyl; (b) W2: 2-(4-nitrophenyl); (c) W3: 2-(4-bromophenyl); (d) D4: 2-(4-diphenylaminophenyl); (e) D5: 2-(4-aminophenyl); (f) W6: 2-(pyridine-4-yl).
Schematic illustration of energy conversion processes of Ir(III) complex–GQD nanocomposites.

Absorption spectra of Ir(III) complex–GQD nanocomposites with different 2-aryl substituents, i.e., (a) R1: 2-phenyl; (b) W2: 2-(4-nitrophenyl); (c) W3: 2-(4-bromophenyl); (d) D4: 2-(4-diphenylaminophenyl); (e) D5: 2-(4-aminophenyl); (f) W6: 2-(pyridine-4-yl).

Calculated binding energy (α), dipole moment (DM), chemical hardness (β), chemical potential (γ) and electro-donating power (ζ) of isolated GQD, isolated Ir(III) complex and Ir(III) complex–GQD nanocomposites. The calculations are conducted in dichloromethane.

The calculated excitation energies, oscillator strengths (fosc), eigenvector analysis of the absorption maxima (i.e., S1, S2, and S3; λmax (eV)), and charge transfer lengths (ΔL) of the lowest excited states of Ir(III) complex–GQD nanocomposites with different 2-aryl substituents in dichloromethane.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note: ILCT: intraligand charge transfer; LLCT: ligand-to-ligand charge transfer; MLCT; metal-to-ligand charge transfer.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Total hyperpolarizability (κtotal) of Ir(III) complex–GQD nanocomposite attached with different electron-withdrawing and -donating substituents.
Energy conversion efficiency and related photovoltaic parameters.

head.ce:title: Investigation of the photocatalytic properties of Ir(III) complex-graphene quantum dot nanocomposites using density functional theory calculations

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of New Materials and Shoes & Clothing Engineering, Liming Vocational University, Quanzhou 362000, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of New Materials and Shoes & Clothing Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of New Materials and Shoes & Clothing Engineering, Liming Vocational University, Quanzhou 362000, People's Republic of China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanotechnology Research Laboratory, Jiangnan University, 1800 Lihu Ave, Binhu District, Wuxi 214122, People's Republic of China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Nanotechnology Research Laboratory, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, Jiangsu Province, P.R.China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The current study investigates the photocatalytic properties of Ir(III) complex–graphene quantum dot (GQD) nanocomposites for potential applications in photocatalysis using density functional theory calculations. The 2-aryl substituent with strong electron-accepting strength leads to better charge transfer ability of the nanocomposite, while the one with strong electron-donating strength redshifts absorption peaks. The energy alignment between the Ir(III) complex and the GQD creates favorable conditions for charge injection to TiO2. Moreover, the electron-donating substituent increases the delocalization of π-electrons in the nanocomposite, enhancing the number of charge carriers injected to TiO2. Furthermore, the electron-donating group increases the charge carrier density of excited states and thus the light collection efficiency (LHE) of the nanocomposite. In contrast, the electron-withdrawing group reduces the LHE and thus the photoelectric conversion of the nanocomposite. The current study depicts the detailed role of electronegativity of the substituted functional group on the energy conversion process of Ir(III) complex–GQD nanocomposites.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The raw/processed data required to reproduce these findings cannot be shared at this time due to technical or time limitations.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a class of zero-dimensional (0 D) quantum-confined graphene, have garnered significant interest due to their exceptional properties [1]. These graphene nanosheets possess a size smaller than 100 nm, rendering them significantly smaller than conventional quantum dots (QDs), such as metal oxides or metal sulfides. GQDs are used for a variety of photovoltaic and photocatalytic applications such as solar cells [2,3] and electrophotocatalytic cells [4,5]. GQDs can improve light absorption and charge separation efficiency, enabling more efficient energy conversion. They also have promising potential for photoelectrochemical water splitting, degradation of pollutants, and other environmental applications. Thanks to their tunable bandgap, they can easily absorb different wavelengths of sunlight, thus increasing the light collection efficiency. Moreover, GQDs are better than the conventional II-VI quantum dots (QDs) because the carbon framework is nontoxic, while the II-VI QDs contain heavy elements that make them toxic to the environment. In addition, the excellent mechanical properties and high carrier mobility of GQDs make them outstanding candidates for photovoltaic applications. The photovoltaic performance of GQDs is related to their electronic structure, which is strongly influenced by the heterogeneity of GQDs. In particular, doping and chemical functionalization are two commonly used methods to optimize the electronic properties of GQDs. In the first method, additional energy levels are inserted into the frontier molecular orbitals (FMOs), varying the photoluminescence emission energy [6–8]. The second method changes the delocalization of π electrons in GQDs, varying electron-hole recombination dynamics [9–11].

d6 Ir(III) complexes have broad absorption spectral range. They also exhibit higher thermal and chemical stability, making them promising candidates for photocatalysis [12,13], nonlinear optics [14,15], bioimaging [16,17], and light-emitting diodes [18,19]. In contrast to the size tunability of QD materials, the Ir(III) complexes depend on coordination ligands [20]. Moreover, the spin-orbit coupling effect causes inert electron-hole recombination kinetics in the Ir(III) complex, which extends the carrier lifetimes and thus increases the charge injection efficiency. The electronic structures of Ir(III) complexes can be modified by selective design of their cyclometalating and diimine ligands, allowing efficient tuning of their excited-state properties. This can be done by either changing the substituents or by π-conjugating the two ligands. More specifically, the phenanthroline ligand with 2-aryl imidazole is the most commonly used diimine ligand [21,22] that can be tuned to different electronic and optical properties by varying the 2-aryl substituents (Fig. 1 ). However, the repulsive force between the 2-H hydrogen bond and the NH hydrogen bond disrupts the planarity of the imidazole ring, affecting the tunability of the electronic structure of the complex. This problem can be circumvented by replacing the imidazole ring with an oxazole ring [23], which is known to be more coplanar with the 2-aryl substituent. Nevertheless, the exact mechanism of the tunability of the 2-aryl substituent to the optoelectronic properties of the Ir(III) complex remains unclear.

GQD–dye nanocomposites can enhance the photoabsorption of the dye in the visible spectrum, leading to increased photoelectric conversion efficiency [24–27]. The combination of dye and GQDs solves this problem by hybridizing the dye with GQDs, since the GQDs have a tunable band gap. For example, Saedi et al. prepared dye-GQD nanocomposites by extracting dyes from natural plants [28]. The as-prepared dye-GQD nanocomposites have an energy conversion efficiency of 0.94 %. Mandal et al. investigated the electronic properties of porphyrin-GQD nanocomposites with potential applications in solar cells [29]. Gao et al. observed that graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs)-tetraphenylporphyrin (TPP) nanocomposites have favorable electronic structures for improved light absorption efficiency in the visible spectrum [30]. Despite the numerous studies on dye-GQD nanocomposites, there are no reports on Ir(III) complex–GQD nanocomposites. Compared with a dye, Ir(III) complex shows better absorption and nonlinear photoabsorption in the visible spectrum, resulting in improved light collection efficiency and charge separation upon photoexcitation. Therefore, it may be more suitable for hybridization with GQD to produce nanocomposite materials that can be used as sensitizers for photovoltaic applications.

The photovoltaic properties, especially the energy conversion efficiency, play a key role in photocatalytic reaction processes [31,32]. To achieve better photocatalytic performance, more photons must be converted into electrons. The photovoltaic parameters such as open circuit voltage (Voc), short-circuit current density (Jsc), and fill factor (FF) are crucial for the performance of a light sensitizer [33]. When the values of Voc and Jsc are higher, the photoelectric conversion rate is improved, resulting in higher photocatalytic efficiency.

This research investigates the electronic and optical characteristics of Ir(III) complex–GQD nanocomposites based on density functional theory (DFT) calculations. A 2-aryl substituent that can either accept or donate electrons is placed on the oxazole ring of the phenanthroline ligand of the Ir(III) complex. To investigate how the change of the substituent affects the photoelectric conversion efficiency of the GQD–Ir(III) complex nanocomposite, we calculate the Voc and the Jsc of the nanocomposite and analyze the relevant parameters affecting them. This is the first systematic study of a Ir(III) complex–GQD nanocomposite to our knowledge. The results of this research may be useful for the rational development of a photovoltaic device using Ir(III) complex–GQD nanocomposites.

The geometric structure of the nanocomposites was prepared by linking the GQDs to the cyclometalating ligand of the Ir(III) complex via an amide bond [34]. Li et al. have investigated the absorption mode of amidation bonding in comparison with two alternative modes, radical addition and cycloaddition of an organic molecule with GQD, using the DFT calculations. The results of their comprehensive study show that the amidation bond undoubtedly has the most favorable photoconversion efficiency compared to the other two modes [35]. A total of six Ir(III) complexes with different chemical groups substituted at the 2-position of the aryl ring (i.e., 2-aryl substituent) were attached to the GQDs, as shown in Fig. 1. We use the letter R1 to denote the pristine Ir(III) complexes (Ir(III) complex:R1), i.e., the Ir(III) complex without bound 2-aryl substituents, and the pristine Ir(III) complex–GQD nanocomposites (Ir(III) complex–GQD:R1), i.e., the Ir(III) complex–GQD nanocomposite without bonded 2-aryl substituents, where R1 represents the aryl ring, i.e., R1 = 2-aryl. The letter W and D represent the electron-withdrawing and -donating groups, respectively. In total, we have five different Ir(III) complex–GQD nanocomposites, namely Ir(III) complex–GQD:W2 (W2 = 2-(4-nitrophenyl)), Ir(III) complex–GQD:W3 (W3 = 2-(4-bromophenyl)), Ir(III) complex–GQD:D4 (D4 = 2-(4-diphenylaminophenyl)), Ir(III) complex–GQD:D5 (2-(4-aminophenyl)) and Ir(III) complex–GQD:W6 (2-(pyridin-4-yl)). In addition, the isolated GQD (C47NH19O) and Ir(III) complexes were also constructed for comparison.

DFT and time-dependent DFT (TD-DFT) were used to calculate the optoelectronic properties of Ir(III) complex–GQD nanocomposites in dichloromethane. For nanocomposites containing a transition metal, a thorough analysis of static and dynamic correlations is required. For this purpose, a multiconfiguration wavefunction approach is usually used, such as the multiconfiguration wavefunction SCF (CASSCF) [36,37], the multireference configuration interaction (MR-CI) [38,39], the restricted active space self-consistent field (RASSCF) [40,41], and the second-order perturbation method [42,43]. However, the high computational cost of these approaches makes it impractical to simulate large systems with hundreds of atoms. In contrast, DFT and TDDFT are much more practical methods for simulating the fundamental optical properties of closed systems such as d6 transition metal complexes [21,22] and QDs [44,45]. Since the d6 for Ir(III) has a complete occupation of the three lowest t2g states and is not a partial occupation, the TDDFT can provide a qualitatively accurate description of the Ir(III) complex–GQD system with affordable computational cost [46]. The 6-31 g* and LANL2DZ basis sets are applied to non-metallic elements and Ir, respectively. Reports suggest that the LANL2DZ basis set accurately describes the electronic structure of Ir [22,47]. The calculations are conducted in dichloromethane solvent with the conductor-like polarizable continuum model (CPCM) [48].

The binding energy between Ir(III) complex and GQD can affect the stability and durability of the hybrid material under photocatalytic conditions. Strong binding between the components can enhance the mechanical stability of the hybrid material, preventing degradation or detachment of the photocatalytic component during photocatalysis. This can help to maintain high photocatalytic activity over extended periods. The binging energy (α) is given by [46]:
(1)
α =
E nanocomposite
−
E
Ir
III
complex
−
E GQD
where
E nanocomposite
is the total energy of Ir(III) complex − GQD nanocomposites;
E
Ir
III
complex
is the total energy of isolated Ir(III) complex and
E GQD
is the total energy of isolated GQD.

The chemical reactivity of the photocatalyst depends on the chemical hardness (β), chemical potential (γ), and electro-donating power (ζ) [22]. The β is a measure of the resistance of a molecule or material to undergoing a chemical reaction. It is defined as the energy required to remove or add an electron from the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) or lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of a molecule, respectively. A molecule with high chemical hardness will require a lot of energy to undergo a reaction, while a molecule with low chemical hardness will be more reactive and easier to undergo a reaction. In the context of photocatalysis, photocatalysts with lower chemical hardness are generally more reactive and can more easily undergo photocatalytic reactions. γ indicates how well the molecule can attract electrons, while ζ refers to the ability of a molecule or atom to donate electrons to other molecules or atoms in a chemical reaction. In general, substances with smaller value of ζ tend to be more reactive in chemical reactions because they are able to donate electrons to other molecules or atoms, leading to the formation of new chemical bonds. In the context of photocatalysis, ζ can affect the chemical reactivity of a photocatalyst. For example, a photocatalyst with higher ζ can more effectively inject electrons to the TiO2, leading to a more efficient reduction reaction. Therefore, smaller values of γ and ζ can increase the charge injection [49,50]. The calculation details of β, γ and ζ are provided in section I in the supplemental materials.

The energy conversion efficiency (ξ) of the light sensitizer and depends on Voc and Jsc according to the following equation:
(2)
ξ = FF
V oc
J sc
P in
where Pin is the incoming solar power.

The methodology for calculating FF and Voc can be found in [51]. In particular, the Voc is determined by the energy gap between the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of the Ir(III) complex–GQD nanocomposite and the conduction band minimum (CBM) of TiO2 [33].

The formula of calculating Jsc is shown as follows:
(3)
J sc
= ∫ LHE
λ
ϕ inj
ϱ collect
dλ
where ϕinj stands for the efficiency of electron injection [52];
ϱ collect
is the charge collection efficiency [53]; LHE is the light collection efficiency [33]. A larger ∆Ginj increases the charge carrier density at the LUMO of TiO2 and thus increases the ϕinj; a larger ∆Greg decreases the electron back transfer and thus increases the
ϱ collect
. The methos of calculating ΔGinj and ΔGreg can be found in references [52,53].

The 2-aryl substituent and the oxazole ring are in the same plane in Ir(III) complex–GQD nanocomposites, except for the diphenyl substituent, where there is an dihedral angle of about 45° between the two benzene rings, consistent with previous reports [54].

The calculated binding energies (α) for the Ir(III) complex-GQD nanocomposites, as shown in Table 1 , range from −4.4 to −4.1 eV, indicating their structural stability in dichloromethane. The predicted band gap of the isolated GQD and Ir(III) complex is 3.54 eV, agreeing with the previous theoretical results [55]. The β-values become smaller when comparing the Ir(III) complex–GQD nanocomposites with the isolated GQDs because the LUMOs of the nanocomposites are dominated by the Ir(III) complex (except for Ir(III) complex–GQD:D4). For the Ir(III) complex–GQD:D4, the charge redistribution between GQD and complex decreases the band gap of the GQD. The β-values of Ir(III) complex–GQD nanocomposites and isolated Ir(III) complexes are reduced compared to their pristine counterparts, and their chemical hardness is affected by the substituents due to changes in FMOs. The electron-donating substituents shift the highest occupied molecular orbitals (HOMOs) of the Ir(III) complexes to higher energy levels. Similarly, the electron-attracting substituents drive the LUMOs of the Ir(III) complexes (see Fig. 3) to higher energy levels - an observation consistent with previous results [56]. If we examine the magnitudes of the HOMO and LUMO shifts, we can see that W2, W6, and W3 are strong electron acceptors with decreased electron attraction, while D4 and D5 are strong electron donors with decreased electron donor power. Moreover, the γ of pristine Ir(III) complex–GQD nanocomposite is lower than those of isolated Ir(III) complexes as well as nanocomposites associated with strong electron-withdrawing substituents (i.e., W2 and W6), while the opposite is true for those associated with electron-donating and weak electron-withdrawing substituents (i.e., D4, D5, and W3). Meanwhile, the ζ of Ir(III) complexes and nanocomposites is significantly enhanced by substituents W2 and W6, while it is only slightly increased or decreased by other substituents, suggesting that strong electron-withdrawing groups can enhance the charge transfer ability and thus chemical reactivity of Ir(III) complex–GQD nanocomposites.

The formation of Ir(III) complex–GQD nanocomposites increases the dipole moment (DMs) compared to those of isolated GQD and Ir(III) complexes, indicating a significant redistribution of charge between GQD and Ir(III) complex once they form nanocomposites. With the exception of D5 (i.e., 2-(4-aminophenyl)), the other substituents reduce the DMs of the isolated Ir(III) complexes as well as the Ir(III) complex–GQD nanocomposites compared to their pristine counterparts. Moreover, the binding energies of the Ir(III) complex–GQD nanocomposites decrease after binding with the 2-aryl substituents.

Fig. 2 shows a fascinating discovery when we examine the nanocomposites: All exhibit a LUMO level higher than the CBM of TiO2 (−4.0 eV [56]) and a HOMO level lower than the HOMO level of I−/I3− (−4.8 eV [56]). The resulting energy alignment facilitates a crucial aspect of charge separation. Moreover, an interesting observation arises from the analysis of the LUMO levels of the Ir(III) complex–GQD nanocomposites, which appear to exceed the levels observed in photovoltaic cells sensitized with Ixora flower dye [57] and triazatruxene dye [58]. In contrast, the HOMO values of the nanocomposites are lower than those of the photovoltaic cells sensitized with ixora flower dye and triazatruxene dye. This stark contrast between the Ir(III) complex–GQD nanocomposites and the aforementioned sensitized photovoltaic cells suggests that the formation of Ir(III) complex–GQD nanocomposites provides more robust driving forces for electron injection into the semiconductor and regeneration of the nanocomposite ground state. Such energy levels are incredibly favorable for the use of Ir(III) complex–GQD nanocomposites in photocatalytic applications.

Fig. 3 shows that the HOMO of the nanocomposite is dictated by GQD states, while the LUMO of the nanocomposite is dictated by the complex states, except for the Ir(III) complex–GQD:D4. The electron-withdrawing substituent has a major effect on lowering the energy levels of unoccupied FMOs of the Ir(III) complex, while the electron-donating substituent mainly increases the energy levels of the occupied FMOs of the GQD and the unoccupied FMOs of the Ir(III) complex. The energy alignment between the GQD and the Ir(III) complex is beneficial for charge separation in photovoltaic cells, as illustrated in Fig. 4 . When the GQD is photoexcited, electrons are transferred from its excited state to the unoccupied FMOs of the Ir(III) complex, increasing the carrier density in the LUMO of Ir(III) complex. Moreover, the back electron transfer from the Ir(III) complex to the GQD is much slower than electron injection [59]. Such energetic alignment in metal-organic nanocomposites significantly reduces radiative and nonradiative recombination, which is much slower than in QDs [60–62]. For the Ir(III) complex–GQD:D4, the diphenylaminophenyl increases the HOMO of the Ir(III) complex due to the strong electron-donating ability, resulting in the HOMO of the nanocomposite being determined by the Ir(III) complex states. This energy alignment favors the hole extraction out of GQD and increases the charge injection efficiency from the GQD to the Ir(III) complex due to the reduced electron-hole recombination in GQD. In the meanwhile, the oxidized Ir(III) complex can be immediately reduced by the redox pair I−/I3−.

The polar solvent increases the DMs of the isolated GQD, Ir(III) complexes and their nanocomposites (supplemental Table S1), indicating increased charge redistribution by the polar solvent. The β values of the isolated Ir(III) complexes and their nanocomposites also increase in polar solvents, whereas the β of the isolated GQD remains the same regardless of solvent, indicating that the FMOs of the GQD are insensitive to the solvent. Additionally, the γ- and ζ-values of the isolated Ir(III) complexes and their nanocomposites increase with the use of polar solvents, showing that nonpolar solvents are better suited to improve the chemical reactivity of the nanocomposites.

The photoelectric conversion also depends on the thermal loss of the lowest excited state. Fast nonradiative recombination leads to thermal losses and a reduction in solar energy conversion. We calculate the charge transfer lengths (ΔL) to characterize the nonradiative recombination. The data from Table 2 show that the addition of 2-aryl substituents to the Ir(III) complex–GQD nanocomposites increases the ΔL, resulting in a weaker electron-hole interaction, which in turn leads to slower nonradiative recombination. The order of ΔL of nanocomposites with different electron-withdrawing substituents is: W2 > W6 > W3, while for nanocomposites with electron-donating substituents the order is D4 > D5, which is consistent with the HOMO and LUMO shifts of nanocomposites. Therefore, increasing the electron acceptor or electron donor strength of the substituent facilitates the charge separation in the nanocomposites and reduces the nonradiative recombination.

The nonlinear optical properties are crucial for characterizing the charge transfer capability of the light sensitizer since efficient electron injection from the Ir(III) complex–GQD nanocomposite onto the semiconductor requires a polarized π-electron system with significant π-electron delocalization. From PDOS, we know that for all nanocomposites, the HOMOs are localized at the GQD and the LUMOs are localized at the complex, except for the Ir(III) complex–GQD:D4, where the HOMO is localized at the diimine ligand. This feature indicates the occurrence of a significant charge transfer excitation in Ir(III) complex–GQD nanocomposites. The lowest excited state of Ir(III) complex–GQD:D4 nanocomposite is a charge transfer state, as indicated by the ΔL of more than 2 Å, although both its LUMO and HOMO are within the Ir(III) complex. The total hyperpolarizability (κtot) [63] of the isolated Ir(III) complexes and their nanocomposites were calculated to characterize the degree of electron delocalization (Table 3 ) and the calculation details of κtot are provided in section I of supplemental materials. In general, the κtot values of the nanocomposites are higher than those of the corresponding Ir(III) complexes without GQD. This suggests that the GQD enhances the nonlinear optical properties of the nanocomposites. The pristine Ir(III) complex–GQD nanocomposite showed a larger κtot than the pristine Ir(III) complex and nanocomposites bound with electron-withdrawing substituents, indicating that the formation of the pristine Ir(III) complex–GQD nanohybrid increases the overall π-electron delocalization in the as-prepared nanocomposites compared to the other nanocomposites with electron-withdrawing substituents. Moreover, the strong electron-withdrawing group increases the κtot, while the strong electron-donating group decreases with the κtot. This indicates that the 2-aryl substituent with a strong electron-withdrawing ability enhances the charge separation and thus the electron injection in the Ir(III) complex–GQD nanocomposite, while the trend is opposite for the 2-aryl substituent with strong electron-donating ability. For the Ir(III) complex–GQD:D5, the dipolar interaction between GQD and the Ir(III) complex causes a significant charge redistribution in the nanocomposite, resulting in the largest κtot, indicating that the electron-donating substituent increases the charge transfer ability of the nanocomposite.

The photovoltaic performance of nanocomposite is associated with photoelectric conversion. The higher the energy conversion efficiency, the more electron-hole pairs are generated. The absolute value of ΔGinj must be larger to allow ultrafast injection of photoexcited electrons from the nanocomposite to TiO2, which requires rapid regeneration of the nanocomposite ground state to slow recombination back to the HOMO of the Ir(III) complex–GQD. As mentioned earlier, HOMO is determined by the GQD, while the LUMO is determined by the Ir(III) complex. This means that the decrease in the electron-attracting ability of the 2-aryl substituent increases the efficiency of electron injection and the reduction efficiency of the oxidized nanocomposite increases with an increased electron-donating ability of the 2-aryl substituent, as can be seen in the PDOS (Fig. 3).

The Ir(III) complex–GQD:D5 nanocomposite has the highest energy conversion efficiency (η) among all nanocomposites due to its larger Voc and Jsc (Table 4 ). The higher Jsc of Ir(III) complex–GQD:D5 is due to its higher LHE compared to the other nanocomposites, as shown by the larger intensity of the S3 state. In addition, the larger delocalization of π-electrons indicated by the total hyperpolarizability contributes to the fast electron injection to the semiconductor. The η of Ir(III) complex–GQD:D4 is smaller than that of Ir(III) complex–GQD:R1 due to its smaller ΔGreg, resulting in lower reduction driving force and slower reduction of the oxidized nanocomposites to the ground state. The η of the Ir(III) complex–GQD nanocomposites with electron-withdrawing substituents are η(Ir(III) complex-GQD:W6) > η(Ir(III) complex-GQD:W2) > η(Ir(III) complex-GQD:W3) and are all smaller than that of the pristine nanocomposite. The Ir(III) complex–GQD:W6 nanocomposite has the largest energy conversion efficiency among the three nanocomposites because its ΔGreg is larger than those of the other two nanocomposites. Although the Ir(III) complex–GQD:W3 has a higher Voc than the other two nanocomposites, it also has a smaller ΔGreg, resulting in a slower recovery of its ground state. In addition, the ΔL of Ir(III) complex–GQD:W3 is smaller than those of the other two nanocomposites, while its total hyperpolarizability is smaller than that of Ir(III) complex–GQD:W2, indicating a larger nonradiative electron-hole recombination and a lower π-electron delocalization, which also leads to a lower photoelectric conversion. As a result, the η of Ir(III) complex–GQD:W3 is smaller than those of the other nanocomposites.

The determined η-values of Ir(III) complex–GQD nanocomposites in this study show a significant insignificance, as they exhibit a very low value of less than 1 %, which is notably remarkable and striking. In comparison, other reported sensitizers have provided much better results. For example, Zhao et al. reported an impressive photoconversion efficiency of 17.4 % for triazatruxene dye–GQD nanocomposites and attributed this performance to the enhanced intermolecular charge transfer and regeneration mechanisms [58]. Similarly, Kar et al. achieved a maximum photoconversion efficiency of 10.82 % with CdX (X = Se, Te) QD/P3HT composites [64]. However, in this study, mainly the Ir(III) complex–GQD nanocomposites are used for photocatalysis. In photocatalysis, a semiconductor material is used to absorb light and generate electron-hole pairs that can drive chemical reactions. The efficiency with which this process occurs is an important factor, as it determines the amount of chemical product that can be generated per unit of light absorbed. However, other factors such as the ability of the catalyst to selectively promote the desired reaction over other competing reactions, separate charges and its stability under reaction conditions, are also important. The Ir(III) complex–GQD nanocomposite exhibits unique catalytic properties because its LUMO and HOMO levels are determined by the Ir(III) complex and GQD, respectively. Therefore, this nanocomposite can be utilized for multiple purposes. Additionally, the energetic alignment at the interface between the Ir(III) complex and GQD favors charge separation in nanocomposites. Moreover, the unique properties of GQDs, such as their strong photoluminescence and high electrical conductivity, make them promising candidates for use in photoelectronic devices. The excellent stability and low toxicity of GQDs also make them ideal for use in biomedical applications such as bioimaging and drug delivery. Furthermore, the energy conversion efficiency of Ir(III) complex–GQD nanocomposites can be improved by heteroatom doping [44] and surface functionalization [51] of the GQDs, which increases the light collection efficiency of the nanocomposites in the infrared region. Research in this area will be continued in the future.

The current work investigates the electronic structure, linear and nonlinear optical properties, and photovoltaic performance of Ir(III) complex–GQD nanocomposites for their potential applications in photocatalysis. The formation of Ir(III) complex–GQD nanohybrids leads to a decrease in chemical hardness due to the energy alignment and charge redistribution between Ir(III) complex and GQD. In particular, the increase in electron-accepting and -donating strength of the substituents has dominant effects on the HOMO and LUMO levels of the Ir(III) complex. Moreover, the strong electron-withdrawing substituent leads to a reduction of chemical potentials and increases the electron-donating powers of Ir(III) complex–GQD nanocomposite, which improves its chemical reactivity. Polar solvent decreases the chemical reactivity of the Ir(III) complex–GQD nanocomposite.

The electron-donating substituent shifted the absorption spectra, while the electron-withdrawing substituent changed only the absorption intensity of the nanocomposite. Both types of substituents changed the transition characters of the main absorption peaks, as shown by the orbital contributions. Furthermore, increasing the electron-withdrawing or -donating strength of the substituent resulted in higher charge separation between electrons and holes and lower nonradiative recombination. In addition, the electron-donating substituent increased the delocalization of π-electrons in the nanocomposite. However, increasing the electron-donating strength of the substituent decreases the extent of delocalization.

The electron-withdrawing substituent decreases the photoelectric conversion of the nanocomposite due to the decreased short-circuit current density and open-circuit voltage, while the strong electron-donating substituent significantly increases the light collection efficiency and thus the short-circuit current density; therefore, it increases the photoelectric efficiency of the nanocomposite. However, the energy conversion efficiency decreases as the electron-donating strength increases, which causes the lower short-circuit current density.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.diamond.2023.109906.

[62] B.W. Pfennig, Principles of inorganic chemistry, John Wiley & Sons, 2021.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Non-linear optical properties of novel fluorenyl derivatives—ab initio quantum chemical calculations

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: [63] K.S. Thanthiriwatte, K.N. De Silva, Non-linear optical properties of novel fluorenyl derivatives—ab initio quantum chemical calculations. Journal of Molecular Structure: THEOCHEM, 617 (2002) 169-175.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly efficient inorganic-organic heterojunction solar cells based on polymer and CdX (X= se, Te) quantum dots: an insight from a theoretical study

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: [64] M. Kar, S. Sarkar, P. Sarkar, Highly Efficient Inorganic–Organic Heterojunction Solar Cells Based on Polymer and CdX (X= Se, Te) Quantum Dots: An Insight from a Theoretical Study. The Journal of Physical Chemistry C, 124 (2020) 11350-11357.
